nativeRange.habitat: Shallow warm coastal waters, tide pools, reef flats, and lagoons. Found in all oceans except the Arctic.

avoidWith: aggressive tang and herbivore fish if display use — tangs and other herbivores will consume the algae

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 5184x3888. In-situ reef photo of a vibrant deep-red bushy Gracilaria sp. clump on the reef — sharp, whole plant in frame, exactly the ornamental red-macroalgae look.
Emoody26; 2272x1704. Bright red branching Gracilaria sp. growing on live rock in a reef aquarium — sharp, vivid colour, shows the hobbyist display context.
Voctir; 4000x3000. Sharp whole-thallus shot of a single reddish Gracilaria sp. showing the cartilaginous branching morphology clearly; out of water but crisp and well-lit.

summary: Thick Ogo is an umbrella name for numerous Gracilaria trade forms — branching and ornamental red macroalgae sold under names such as Pom Pom Gracilaria, Red Ogo, and Bird's Nest Gracilaria. The genus is widely used in refugiums for nutrient export and as a highly palatable food for tangs and other marine herbivores.
careNotes:
This entry covers the Gracilaria genus as a whole. Several species within this genus that have confirmed species-level identification are covered separately: [red-ogo] (G. parvispora), [pom-pom] (G. hayi), [green-ogo-gracilaria-tikvahiae] (G. tikvahiae), [red-spaghetti] (G. pacifica), and [monkey-finger] (G. salicornia).

Gracilaria is one of the most widely cultivated macroalgae genera in the reef aquarium hobby. It tends to grow rapidly in well-lit, high-flow refugiums and is generally considered among the best choices for nutrient export in reef tanks. Clippings can be fed directly to tangs, surgeonfish, and other marine herbivores as a highly palatable food source.

The genus is distributed across all major tropical and subtropical oceans, absent from Arctic waters. It generally prefers water temperatures above 22°C and may decline at lower temperatures. Typical reef water parameters apply: temperature 22–28°C, salinity SG 1.023–1.025, pH 7.8–8.4, alkalinity dKH 8–12. Trace supplementation with iron and iodine has been reported to support growth when natural aquarium nutrients are insufficient, though this is rarely necessary in established reef systems.

Refugium placement is most common — tumbling freely in strong flow under a grow light. Gracilaria can also be placed in display tanks as an ornamental red alga, though tangs and other herbivores will consume it readily if given access. Propagation is by simple fragmentation; trimming to harvest for tang feeding also naturally controls growth.

As a red alga, Gracilaria will MELT at the base if buried in sand — it should be attached to rock using superglue, zip-tie or fishing line, or wedged into a crevice instead. Sand-bed planting is generally suited to green algae genera such as Caulerpa, Halimeda, Penicillus, Rhipocephalus, and Udotea, as well as some calcified Dasycladales.
breedingNotes: Gracilaria is propagated in captivity by thallus fragmentation — trimming a section of the alga and allowing it to establish elsewhere in the system. Gametophyte and sporophyte alternation (the alga's sexual reproductive cycle in the wild) is not attempted in aquariums. Regular trimming both harvests material for feeding and naturally keeps growth in check.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics — Red Ogo Gracilaria
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Page confirms refugium + flow use and nutrient export but lacks detailed water parameter specs. Water parameters from AlgaeBarn primary.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics — Pom Pom Gracilaria
additional.notes: Additional TSA Gracilaria sp. listing — confirms refugium tumbling, ample flow, nutrient export (nitrates, phosphates, DOC), and compatibility note that tangs/herbivores will consume it.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Bird's Nest Gracilaria
additional.notes: Mosaic Macros Gracilaria sp. listing. Confirms red branching morphology. Care parameters not stated; image candidate sourced.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Gracilaria
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmed: Family Gracilariaceae, Order Gracilariales, Class Florideophyceae, Division Rhodophyta. Distribution: all oceans except Arctic; cannot survive below 10°C. AlgaeBase blocked by CAPTCHA verification — Wikipedia used as taxonomy fallback.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
